channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Man Held in Hawaii for 2 Years Over Mistaken Identity Gets $975,000 Settlement
item.description: After spending over two years in a psychiatric hospital, Joshua Spriestersbach, 54, should no longer face the risk of being rearrested or jailed for the crimes of the person he was mistaken for.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Ziegler

item.title: House Ethics Panel Holds Rare Public Hearing on Democrat’s Conduct
item.description: Representative Sheila Cherfilus-McCormick of Florida is charged with stealing $5 million in FEMA money for her campaign.
item.dc:creator: Annie Karni
item.media:description: Representative Sheila Cherfilus-McCormick on Thursday. Her hearing was the first time in 16 years that the typically secretive House Ethics Committee had held a public hearing regarding the actions of a sitting lawmaker.

item.title: Kennedy’s Vaccine Agenda Hits Roadblocks, Diminishing His Clout
item.description: Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr.’s vaccine skepticism is posing challenges for the Trump administration. Top health jobs are unfilled, and a court has blocked his vaccine schedule changes.
item.dc:creator: Sheryl Gay Stolberg
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:description: Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr.’s vaccine agenda — his insistence that vaccines are somehow linked to autism, despite a lack of evidence — has always been a bit of an Achilles’ heel.

item.title: Trump Says He Will Sign Order to Pay T.S.A. Agents as Travel Frustrations Grow
item.description: President Trump said he would sign an emergency order to pay Transportation Security Administration agents, with an intensifying crisis at airports ahead of a busy travel weekend.
item.dc:creator: Chris Cameron and Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump said on Thursday that he would sign an emergency order to pay Transportation Security Administration agents.

item.title: D.H.S. Funding Lapse Leads to Longest Partial Shutdown in History
item.description: If the shutdown continues after this weekend, it will be longer than any previous shutdown, partial or full.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Wu

item.title: California Renames Cesar Chavez Holiday After Sex Abuse Revelations
item.description: Gov. Gavin Newsom signed legislation to rename Cesar Chavez Day as “Farmworkers Day,” moving quickly before the March 31 holiday.
item.dc:creator: Laurel Rosenhall
item.media:description: Cesar Chavez in Salinas, Calif., in 1979.

item.title: Sheriff in California Seizes More Ballots, Ignoring State Attorney General
item.description: Chad Bianco, the Riverside County sheriff and a Republican running for governor, is examining votes from 2025 after unsubstantiated claims of fraud.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Eric Thayer/Los Angeles Times, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Chad Bianco, the sheriff of Riverside County, is locked in a sprawling all-party primary for governor in California, a heavily Democratic state.

item.title: Trump Says He Will Order T.S.A. Agents Paid as Funding Deal Stalls
item.description: President Trump’s announcement came as talks on Capitol Hill over funding the Department of Homeland Security faltered, and airport lines continued to grow.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold and Megan Mineiro

item.title: Trump’s Signature Is Set to Be Added to America’s Currency
item.description: President Trump is poised to be the first sitting president to have his signature appear on the U.S. dollar.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The addition of President Trump’s signature to dollars is the latest example of his emblazoning national institutions with his personal brand.

item.title: G.O.P. Senator Weighs Forcing Congress to Vote to Authorize the Iran War
item.description: Senator Lisa Murkowski of Alaska is drafting a formal authorization for the use of military force in Iran, seeking to put some parameters around the operation as the Trump administration has boxed out Congress.
item.dc:creator: Megan Mineiro and Robert Jimison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Lisa Murkowski of Alaska is one of the few Republicans who raised questions in the early weeks of the war about the scope of the combat operations launched on Feb. 28.

item.title: Standoff With Iran Raises Fresh Doubts About Trump’s Freestyle Diplomacy
item.description: A jumble of emissaries — a friend, a family member, a dove and a hawk — on the Iran crisis reflects President Trump’s improvisational approach.
item.dc:creator: Michael Crowley
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: As the war stretches on, President Trump appears to be casting about for a diplomatic offramp even as he threatens an escalation.

item.title: Stench of 20 Tons of Spilled Tofu Hits Missouri Town ‘Like a Brick Wall’
item.description: A truck careened into a ravine in Jerome, Mo., leaving the tofu to stew for weeks. “It was worse than a landfill on a hot July day,” said the general manager of a towing company.
item.dc:creator: Michael Levenson
item.media:description: An insurance snafu delayed the cleanup of the spill, leaving the tofu stewing, before flash floods pushed some of it into a creek.

item.title: What’s on Trump’s Mind During a Cabinet Meeting? Sharpies, Iran and Inflation.
item.description: President Trump waxed on about the virtues of the pen, calling it more economical and a better instrument than the fancier writing tools preferred by his predecessors.
item.dc:creator: Linda Qiu
item.media:description: President Trump spent several minutes during Thursday’s cabinet meeting at the White House talking about the Sharpie pen he uses and comparing it to pens used by previous presidents.

item.title: Trump Defends His Use of Mail-In Voting: ‘Because I’m President’
item.description: President Trump wants to restrict voting by mail, which he says amounts to “cheating.” But he defended his own use of the practice in a special election this week.
item.dc:creator: Erica L. Green
item.media:description: President Trump defended his use of mail-in voting during a cabinet meeting at the White House on Thursday, despite having referred to the practice as “mail-in cheating.”

item.title: Trump Officials Investigate Stanford, Ohio State and U.C. San Diego Medical Schools
item.description: The Justice Department’s demands for admissions-related data from Stanford, Ohio State and the University of California, San Diego, reveal an expansion of its higher-education pressure campaign.
item.dc:creator: Michael C. Bender and Alan Blinder
item.media:description: Enrollment in medical programs is generally a fraction of that of undergraduate programs. At Stanford University, pictured, the incoming class this academic year had 119 students.

item.title: Trump Eyes White House Treaty Room for Latest Renovation Project
item.description: President Trump is making new plans in one of the most significant renovations in the history of the White House.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater and Maggie Haberman
item.media:credit: Universal History Archive, via Getty Images
item.media:description: President John F. Kennedy signing the Nuclear Test Ban Treaty of 1963 in the Treaty Room. President Trump spoke last month to a group including some members of the Committee for Preservation of the White House and the Commission of Fine Arts about potentially turning the Treaty Room into a bedroom.

item.title: Lawmakers Press to Limit Prediction Bets by Policymakers
item.description: New bills aim to crack down on prediction-market bets by the president and members of Congress.
item.dc:creator: Megan Mineiro
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: People standing near a police station in Tehran that was damaged in U.S.-Israeli attacks. Over a hundred people made more than $10,000 by betting that the United States would strike Iran on Feb. 28.

item.title: Roman Catholic Churches See a Surge of New Converts
item.description: Bishops are trying to understand what’s behind the wave. People joining the church described their reasons as highly personal.
item.dc:creator: Elizabeth Dias
item.media:credit: Taylor Glascock for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Times gathered data from two dozen dioceses. Each reported a significant increase in new converts.

item.title: Four Problems for Trump in Birthright Citizenship Case
item.description: The president must confront a 1952 federal law, the possibility that millions will lose their citizenships, stateless foundlings and a fluid future.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: Protesters stood outside of the Supreme Court last May in support of birthright citizenship.

item.title: After Wooing Trump With Deals, Pakistan Gets a Seat at the Table
item.description: Steve Witkoff, a diplomatic envoy, used the Board of Peace to announce an agreement that could raze a Pakistan-owned Manhattan hotel. Now the country is involved in negotiating peace talks with Iran.
item.dc:creator: David Yaffe-Bellany, Ben Protess and Elian Peltier
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Ahmed Gaber for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Roosevelt Hotel in New York, which is owned by Pakistan, in 2023.

item.title: South Dakota Governor Signs Bill Requiring Citizenship Proof to Vote
item.description: The law, which mirrors national Republican priorities, requires newly registered voters to show that they are U.S. citizens in order to cast a ballot in state or local races.
item.dc:creator: Mitch Smith
item.media:description: Gov. Larry Rhoden of South Dakota at a ceremony in Keystone, S.D., last fall.

item.title: U.S. Military Kills 4 People in Boat Strike in Caribbean
item.description: At least 163 people have been killed in the Trump administration’s campaign against suspected drug smuggling.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt
item.category: US Strikes on Alleged Drug Traffickers (2025- )
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: The Pentagon said it blew up a boat in the Caribbean on Wednesday, the 47th such attack since the campaign started in early September.

item.title: Savannah Guthrie Says 2 Ransom Notes About Her Mother Were Likely Genuine
item.description: Her interview on the “Today” show came more than 50 days after her mother, Nancy Guthrie, was taken from her home near Tucson, Ariz.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Bogel-Burroughs
item.media:description: Savannah Guthrie, left, talking with Hoda Kotb on the “Today” show. It was Ms. Guthrie’s first interview since her mother, Nancy, was abducted.

item.title: Colleges Pressed to Remove Names of Epstein’s Friends From Buildings
item.description: Students and others are asking universities, including Harvard and Ohio State, to take down the names of high-profile donors with connections to Jeffrey Epstein. They have not done so yet.
item.dc:creator: Vimal Patel
item.media:credit: Lucy Lu for The New York Times
item.media:description: The main building of Harvard University’s Kennedy School of Government bears the name of Leslie Wexner, a major benefactor of Mr. Epstein.

item.title: Some Judges See Risks in Fiery Opinions Warning of Threats to Democracy
item.description: Federal judges are weighing strategies for how to respond to the high stakes, anonymous threats and politicized atmosphere of the Trump era.
item.dc:creator: Mattathias Schwartz

item.title: In California’s Central Valley, Latino Voters Are Up for Grabs in 2026’s Elections
item.description: As in other Hispanic areas of the country, voters shifted toward Republicans in 2024. But now the question is whether this was a blip more than a durable trend.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Medina and Ariana Drehsler
